turns.content: hey so i have kind of a dumb question but honestly i've been going back and forth on this for weeks. i do all my design work in figma — like literally everything, ui, branding decks, packaging mockups, the whole thing — and a client just asked me to deliver source files in illustrator format. is there a decent way to export from figma to ai without everything breaking

Not a dumb question at all — this is a really common pain point for Figma-heavy workflows. There's no perfect one-click solution, but here are your best options:

1. **Export as SVG from Figma**, then open in Illustrator. This preserves vector paths, strokes, and most structure. You'll lose some Figma-specific features like auto-layout and component instances, but the geometry stays clean.

2. **Use a plugin like "Figma to AI"** — there are a few community plugins that attempt a more direct conversion. Results vary, but worth trying for simpler files.

3. **Export as PDF** — Figma's PDF export is actually quite good for preserving layout fidelity. Illustrator opens PDFs natively and you get editable vectors.

For packaging mockups especially, I'd go the SVG route and do a cleanup pass in Illustrator. What kind of deliverable is the client expecting — fully editable source files, or more of a handoff?

they want fully editable source files which is like... pain. i've built my entire pipeline around figma honestly. every single project for the last three years has lived there. the collaboration features alone make it impossible to switch, like i can just share a link and the client is looking at the same thing i am in real time

Yeah, that real-time collaboration is genuinely hard to replicate anywhere else. Three years of a fully Figma-native pipeline means your muscle memory, your component libraries, your file organization — everything is optimized for it.

For this specific client, I'd suggest a pragmatic approach:

- Do all your actual design work in Figma as usual
- When it's time to deliver, export the final approved designs as SVG
- Open in Illustrator, reorganize layers to match what they'd expect from a native AI file
- Save as .ai

It adds maybe 30-60 minutes per deliverable, but you're not disrupting your entire workflow. You could even build that time into your quote.

Are they asking for AI files because they have an in-house team that uses Illustrator, or is it more of a "that's what we've always asked for" situation? Sometimes clients are flexible once you explain the benefits of staying in Figma.

it's their in-house marketing team, they're all on adobe creative cloud. honestly fair enough i can't blame them. it's just annoying because figma does everything i need and more. like i even do my initial sketching and wireframing there now, i used to use procreate for that but figma's pen tool got good enough

That's a solid testament to how far Figma's vector tools have come. The fact that you've consolidated even your sketching phase into it means you've really streamlined things.

Since you're dealing with an Adobe-native team, here's another thought: look into **Figma's Dev Mode and the "Copy as SVG" feature** for individual components. Sometimes it's cleaner than a full-page SVG export because you can control exactly what gets flattened vs. kept as paths.

Also, for branding deliverables specifically, you might want to:
- Outline all text before export (fonts are the #1 thing that breaks)
- Flatten any complex boolean operations
- Check that your color profiles are consistent — Figma works in sRGB, and their team might need CMYK if anything's going to print

Do you do much print work, or is it mostly screen-based?

mix of both honestly. i do a lot of packaging design which obviously goes to print, plus ui work and brand identity stuff. being freelance means you kind of take whatever comes through the door right. especially here in osaka the market isn't as big as tokyo so you can't afford to be too picky about what kind of work you do

That makes total sense. Freelancing in a smaller market means being versatile is survival — you become a generalist by necessity even if you have specialist skills. Osaka's creative scene is strong but you're right that the sheer volume of opportunities is different from Tokyo.

For the print side of things, the Figma-to-Illustrator pipeline actually matters more because of CMYK conversion. Here's what I'd recommend:

- In Figma, design in sRGB as usual
- When exporting for print deliverables, go SVG → Illustrator → convert to CMYK using your printer's ICC profile
- Save a template .ai file with your CMYK color swatches pre-loaded so you're not redoing that conversion every time

For UI work you can stay fully in Figma with zero friction since it's all screen color anyway.

Have you considered adding the Illustrator conversion fee as a separate line item on invoices for clients who require .ai files? It's legitimate extra work.

oh that's actually smart, billing it as a separate line item. honestly i'm terrible at pricing my work properly. the feast or famine thing is real, like last month i had five projects overlapping and this month i'm refreshing my email waiting for the next inquiry. makes it hard to push back on anything because you're always thinking what if this is the last client for a while

That feast-or-famine cycle is one of the hardest parts of freelancing, and it absolutely messes with your ability to price confidently. When you're in a famine period, every quote feels like it might scare someone away.

A few things that can help stabilize:

1. **Retainer agreements** — if any of your repeat clients send you work regularly, propose a monthly retainer. Even a small one creates predictable income.

2. **Minimum project fee** — set a floor below which you won't take work. This protects you from undervaluing yourself during slow months.

3. **The "conversion/format" surcharge** is a great start — you can also add line items for things like rush delivery, extra revision rounds, or source file delivery. These are all industry-standard.

4. **Invoice faster** — if you're not already, send invoices immediately upon delivery rather than batching them. Faster invoicing = faster cash flow.

What does your typical client mix look like — mostly local businesses, or do you work with clients elsewhere too?

mostly osaka based clients yeah. some tokyo agencies sub-contract to me for ui stuff but the bread and butter is local businesses. restaurants, retail shops, small startups. they're great to work with honestly but the budgets are... modest. like i designed a whole brand identity for a ramen shop last month and i'm pretty sure my hourly rate worked out to less than what their cooks make lol

Ha — the ramen shop math is painfully relatable for a lot of freelance designers. You pour your heart into crafting the perfect brand identity and the hourly breakdown makes you want to cry.

For local small businesses, a few pricing strategies that work well:

- **Package pricing instead of hourly** — "Brand Identity Package: logo, color palette, business card, menu design — ¥XXX,XXX." Clients understand packages better than hourly rates, and you control the scope.

- **Tiered options** — offer bronze/silver/gold packages. Most clients pick the middle one, and it anchors the conversation around value rather than hours.

- **Portfolio leverage** — small local businesses might pay less, but if the work is good, use it aggressively in your portfolio to attract higher-budget clients. A beautifully designed ramen shop brand can catch a Tokyo agency's eye.

The Tokyo agency sub-contracting is actually your growth lever. Those relationships tend to scale — if you deliver well, they send more work at better rates. Worth investing extra attention there even if the local work pays the immediate bills.

Are you showcasing your work online anywhere — portfolio site, Behance, Dribbble?

i have a portfolio site yeah, built it myself in webflow. it's honestly overdue for an update though, i keep putting it off because every time i sit down to work on it i start redesigning the whole thing from scratch instead of just adding new projects. designer brain is a curse lol

The classic designer trap — redesigning your own site instead of actually updating it. You're definitely not alone in that. It's like a carpenter whose own house is always under renovation.

Honest advice: **set a hard rule — no redesigning, only adding.** Give yourself a two-hour window, drop in your three best recent projects with clean mockup images and a short case study blurb, and hit publish. Done.

If the itch to redesign is too strong, schedule it as a separate project for a slow week. Treat yourself like a client — define scope, set a deadline, stick to it.

For the portfolio itself:
- Lead with the work that attracts the *clients you want*, not just your most recent projects
- If you want more Tokyo agency UI work, put UI projects front and center
- Include a short "about" section — clients like knowing who they're hiring, especially for freelancers

What kind of projects are you hoping to get more of — the UI agency work, or the local branding stuff?

honestly i'd love more of the ui agency work because the rates are way better but i genuinely enjoy the local branding stuff more. like there's something about seeing a logo you designed on an actual physical sign in your neighborhood. i walked past that ramen shop last week and the sign was up and even though i probably made like 800 yen an hour on that project it still felt amazing

That feeling — walking past your work in the physical world — is something UI designers never get. It's a genuinely meaningful part of doing local branding work, and it's worth not losing even as you chase better-paying projects.

Here's a way to have both:

- **Position UI work as your income engine** — pursue agency contracts, optimize your portfolio for that, price it at proper rates
- **Keep local branding as your passion work** — be selective about which projects you take, but when you do them, enjoy the process and the tangible result
- **Over time, raise your local rates gradually.** As your portfolio gets stronger with agency work, local clients will perceive more value. You don't have to price yourself out, but you can inch up.

The ramen shop sign story is actually great portfolio content, by the way. A photo of your design on a real sign in Osaka tells a much better story than a flat mockup. Start documenting that stuff — walk around your neighborhood with your phone and photograph your work in context.

oh i do have a few photos like that actually, i just never thought to put them in the portfolio. that's a good idea though, showing the work in context. do you think case studies with process shots and stuff are worth doing or is that overkill for the kind of clients i'm targeting

Not overkill at all — case studies are actually one of the biggest differentiators between a portfolio that gets inquiries and one that just gets a quick scroll-through.

But the level of detail should match your audience:

**For Tokyo agency prospects:**
- Show process — moodboards, sketches, iterations, final result. Agencies want to know you can *think*, not just push pixels
- Include the problem/brief, your approach, and the outcome
- These don't need to be long — 3-5 images with short captions per project is plenty

**For local business clients:**
- Lead with the final result and the real-world photos
- Keep it visual, less text-heavy
- They want to see "this is what your business could look like" not your design process

You can actually structure both audiences into one portfolio. Lead each project with the hero shots and real-world context photos (catches local clients), then include a "see the process" expandable section or sub-page (catches agencies).

The real-world photos of your work around Osaka are gold for both audiences, honestly. They prove the work is real and lived-in, not just a Dribbble mockup.

okay that dual-audience structure is really smart. i'm going to try that. this whole conversation has covered way more ground than i expected honestly — i came in with a figma export question and now i have a whole pricing and portfolio strategy lol. super helpful though, thank you

Ha — scope creep happens even in conversations. But everything connects: the export issue led to pricing, pricing led to portfolio, portfolio led to positioning. That's actually a good sign that you're thinking about your freelance practice holistically rather than just project-to-project.

Quick recap of your action items:

1. **SVG export → Illustrator cleanup → .ai delivery** for this client
2. **Add format conversion as a line item** on the invoice
3. **Build package pricing tiers** for local business clients
4. **Portfolio update sprint** — two hours, new projects, real-world context photos, no redesign
5. **Dual-audience case studies** — hero shots for local clients, process details for agencies
6. **Lean into the agency pipeline** for income while keeping local branding as creative fuel

The Figma pipeline you've built is clearly working well, so no reason to change the core workflow. Just bolt on the conversion step for Adobe clients and charge for it.

Good luck with the project — come back if you hit any snags with the export or need help thinking through the pricing tiers.
